symbols: scales of justice — the impartial weighing of deeds, divine and human judgment
sword — the authority to execute justice, the power of righteous judgment
theatrical masks below — the dual faces of truth and deception, what justice unmasks
Latin inscription (FODERUNT MEOS) — text from Psalm 22:16, linking justice to Christ's Passion
description: A ceiling fresco detail showing an oval medallion within an ornate architectural surround. Inside the medallion, a standing female figure holds a sword in one hand and scales in the other — the personification of Justice. Above the medallion, a veiled female head crowns the composition. To the sides, dark green drapery is pulled back. Below, two theatrical masks hang from a decorative element. In the lower right corner, a Latin inscription reads 'FODERUNT MEOS DINU...' The surrounding area includes fragments of larger painted figures.
theological_description: This allegory of Justice as a cardinal virtue is enriched by the Latin inscription fragment 'Foderunt meos' — from Psalm 22:16 (Vulgate): 'They pierced my hands and my feet.' This links the abstract concept of justice to the Crucifixion, where divine justice and mercy meet. At the cross, God's righteous judgment against sin and his merciful love for sinners converge in a single act. The scales of justice find their ultimate calibration at Calvary — 'God made him who had no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Corinthians 5:21). The theatrical masks below suggest the unmasking that justice performs — stripping away pretense to reveal truth. Micah's prophetic summary echoes: 'What does the Lord require of you? To act justly and to love mercy and to walk humbly with your God' (Micah 6:8).

scripture_refs.reason: The Latin inscription 'Foderunt meos' references this messianic psalm about piercing hands and feet
God made Christ to be sin so we might become God's righteousness — divine justice fulfilled at the cross
The Lord requires justice, mercy, and humility — the cardinal virtue depicted

_meta.title: Scenes of allegories of the cardinal virtues
